Note: Treatment-emergent adverse events are those that occurred between the date of 1st dose of study drug and date of last dose of study drug +28 days. For each category, subjects are counted only once, even if they experienced multiple events in that category. Write up of Table 1 Almost all subjects in the apalutamide and placebo arms were reported with 1 or more TEAEs. Treatment-emergent adverse events were reported for 97% of subjects in the apalutamide arm and for 93% of subjects in the placebo arm. Grade 3-4 TEAEs were reported for 45% of subjects in the apalutamide arm and for 34% of subjects in the placebo arm; 11% of subjects discontinued treatment due to TEAEs in the apalutamide arm and 7% discontinued treatment due to TEAEs in the placebo arm. Serious adverse events (SAEs) were reported for 25% of subjects in the apalutamide arm and for 23% of subjects in the placebo arm (Table 1). writeup: AEs = adverse events; DB = double-blind; No. = number; OL = open-label; SAE = serious adverse event; TEAE= treatment-emergent Adverse Events. Subjects with brain neoplasm [meningioma] and rectal adenoma had SAEs that began before Day 1 of treatment and were not considered treatment-emergent; therefore these events are not counted here. One subject had an SAE of colon cancer metastatic that began after the subject withdrew consent to participate in the study, so it was considered non-treatment-emergent and does not appear here. One subject had an SAE of dyspnea that began after withdrawal from the study, so it was not considered treatment-emergent and does not appear here. Write up of Table 2 An overall summary of treatment-emergent AEs (TEAEs) that occurred during the DB and OL phases is provided in Table 2.  During the DB phase, the fesoterodine treatment group had a greater proportion of subjects in every category than the placebo group.  However, the rate of serious adverse events (SAEs) was less than 5% for both treatment groups, and only 1 of these was considered treatment-related by the investigator. During the OL phase, greater proportions of subjects who received placebo during the DB phase reported TEAEs, had discontinued from the study due to TEAEs compared to those who received fesoterodine during the DB phase.

writeup: Overall, 128 (74.9%) of 171 subjects in the higher-dose ziprasidone group and 102 (61.8%) of 165 subjects in the lower- dose ziprasidone group reported at least 1 treatment-emergent AE during the study. A total of 68 (13.5%) subject discontinued from the study due to AEs. SAEs were reported by 16 (3.2%) randomized subjects. A total of 10 subjects randomized to ziprasidone reported 15 SAEs; 7 subjects randomized to placebo reported 8 SAEs. Nine of these subjects reposed SAEs that were related to the disease under study. None of these SAEs were considered by the investigator to be related to study treatment. Write up of Table 79 Table 79 below summarizes treatment-emergent, all causality and treatment-related AEs. Of the 147 subjects in each treatment group, 83% of ziprasidone-treated subjects and 73.5% of placebo-treated subjects experienced TEAEs. In the ziprasidone group, 72.8% of subjects had treatment-related AEs; the percentage was lower (46.9%) in the placebo group. There were 8 treatment-emergent serious adverse events (SAEs) reported in 7 subjects; none of these SAEs were treatment-related. All causality Grade 3-4 TEAEs were reported in 18.4% of subjects taking ziprasidone and 7.5% of subjects taking placebo; most of these were considered to be treatment-related in ziprasidone subjects (15.0%), but few were related to study drug in placebo subjects (1.4%). A total of 18.4% of ziprasidone-treated subjects and 10.9% of placebo-treated subjects discontinued from the study due to all causality AEs.

writeup: Of the 123 treated participants in the T2DM population, 79 (64.2%) participants reported TEAEs, of which 57 (46.3%) participants had treatment related TEAEs (Table 200). The proportion of participants with at least 1 all-causality AE in the danuglipron arms ranged from 60.0% to 72.7%, with the lowest proportion in the danuglipron 80 mg BID low, slow arm and the highest proportion in the danuglipron 120 mg BID low, fast arm, compared with 50.0% in the placebo arm. The proportion of participants with at least 1 treatment-related AE in the danuglipron dose arms ranged from 36.4% (in the danuglipron 120 mg BID low, fast arm) to 63.6% (in the danuglipron 120 mg BID high, fast arm), compared with 25.0% in the placebo arm. No SAEs were reported. A total of 5 (4.1%) participants had all-causality Grade 3-4 TEAEs, and 3 (2.4%) participants had treatment-related Grade 3-4 TEAEs. A total of 14 (11.4%) participants discontinued from study due to AEs. Among them 12 (9.8%) participants were due to treatment-related AEs. Non-Diabetic Obesity Participants Of the 28 treated participants in the non-diabetic obesity population, 22 (78.6%) participants reported TEAEs, of which related TEAEs were reported in 18 (64.3%) participants (Table 200). A higher proportion of participants had all-causality AEs in the danuglipron 200 mg BID arm than the placebo arm (81.8% versus 66.7%). Similar results were observed in the proportion of participants with treatment-related AEs. No SAEs were reported. A total of 1 (3.6%) participant had all-causality Grade 3-4 TEAEs, and 1 (3.6%) participant had treatment-related Grade 3-4 TEAEs. A total of 3 (10.7%) participants discontinued from study due to AEs, and all were due to treatment-related AEs. In the non-diabetic obesity arms, a higher proportion of participants had AEs leading to discontinuation from study in the danuglipron 200 mg BID arm than the placebo arm (13.6% versus 0%).
